name: Query Intent
summary: Protocol owning the server-state READ axis (the cross-query cache lifecycle) — symmetric to the Mutation Intent for writes (#1419 Fork 1a). Names the home TanStack Query / SWR / RTK Query / Apollo all converged on: a keyed query cache with fetch policy + staleness. UX-only per the #1419 amendment — it surfaces only fetchPolicy (how a read resolves against the cache) and how stale data is displayed while revalidating; the technical cache machinery (dedupe, fresh/evict windows, revalidation triggers, dependsOn gating) lives off this surface on the swappable resource-cache provider contract (#1460 slice B). Extending loader/resource-loader was rejected (#1419): single-fetch UI is not a cross-query store. Composes loader (the pending/blocking read UX) and the resource-cache provider (the cache).

fetchPolicy.description: How a read resolves against the cache relative to the network — the SWR/Apollo fetchPolicy axis. `cache-first` (default) serves a fresh cache hit without a request and only fetches on a miss/stale entry — the minimal-traffic default. `network-only` always fetches and bypasses the cache read (for always-authoritative views). `cache-and-network` serves the cached value immediately AND fetches in the background, updating when the response lands (the snappiest perceived UX). All three are legitimate end-states (a real dimension), mirroring the near-universal incumbent vocabulary.

staleness.description: How a stale entry (past the provider's fresh window) is presented to the user while a background revalidation runs — the UX of stale-while-revalidate, composing the Loader Intent's pending UX. `silent` (default) serves the stale value immediately with no indicator and swaps in the fresh result when it arrives (the SWR default). `indicator` serves stale plus a subtle revalidating affordance so the user knows a refresh is in flight. `block` suppresses the stale value and shows the loader's pending state until fresh (when showing stale data would mislead). The freshness threshold itself is a provider knob, not a surface concern.

description:
What This Is

The server-state read intent — the cross-query cache lifecycle, symmetric to the Mutation Intent for writes (placement ratified in #1419, Fork 1a). Extending the Loader Intent was rejected: a single-fetch UI is not a cross-query store (the homonym trap #1395 untangled on the write side). Named query, symmetric to useQuery.

UX-only surface (the #1419 amendment)

This intent is UX-only: it exposes only fetch policy and staleness display. The thin cache lifecycle TanStack Query / SWR / RTK Query / Apollo converged on — dedupe, fresh/evict windows, revalidation triggers, dependsOn query-graph gating — is a swappable runtime-DI concern on the resource-cache provider contract (the key → { data, staleness, revalidate } seam, #1460 slice B), never on this surface.

Fetch Policy

How a read resolves against the cache relative to the network — the near-universal SWR/Apollo vocabulary.

  • cache-first: Default. Serve a fresh cache hit with no request; fetch only on a miss/stale entry. Minimal traffic.
  • network-only: Always fetch, bypassing the cache read — for always-authoritative views.
  • cache-and-network: Serve the cached value immediately AND fetch in the background, updating when the response lands. The snappiest perceived UX.

Staleness (composes Loader)

How a stale entry is presented while a background revalidation runs — the UX of stale-while-revalidate, composing the Loader Intent's pending UX.

  • silent: Default. Serve the stale value with no indicator; swap in the fresh result on arrival (the SWR default).
  • indicator: Serve stale plus a subtle revalidating affordance.
  • block: Suppress the stale value and show the loader's pending state until fresh (when stale data would mislead).

Composition (all supported seams, no forks)

  • ↔ loader: the read/write split — loader owns the single-fetch pending/blocking UX; query owns the cross-query cache lifecycle on top of it.
  • ↔ mutation: the write-side symmetric axis — a mutation's reconcile: invalidate marks this intent's cached reads stale.
  • ↔ resource-cache provider (#1460 B): the swappable cache the technical knobs live on; this intent's fetchPolicy drives how the provider's read resolves.

Interface Protocol

type QueryFetchPolicy = 'cache-first' | 'network-only' | 'cache-and-network';
type QueryStaleness = 'silent' | 'indicator' | 'block';

interface QueryIntent {
    // How a read resolves against the cache relative to the network
    fetchPolicy: QueryFetchPolicy;   // default 'cache-first'
    // How a stale entry is displayed while revalidating (composes loader)
    staleness?: QueryStaleness;      // default 'silent'
    // Cache machinery (dedupe, freshWindow, revalidateOn, dependsOn) is NOT here —
    // it lives on the swappable resource-cache provider (#1460 B), off the UX surface.
}
